title: roman_l2_job
text:
roman_l2_job is a self-contained pipeline that simulates Roman WFI Level-2 calibrated images over a chosen footprint, builds Level-3 skycell coadds and source catalogs from them, and measures how faithfully the recovered catalog reproduces the input catalog — a photometry recovery test. It was built to support simulated grism observations of a Wide-Field2 sub-region near (RA=10°, Dec=0°), but generalizes to any footprint and any input source catalog.
Code, configs, and this report live in the roman_l2_job GitHub repo.

title: roman_l2_job
text:
roman_l2_job is a self-contained pipeline that simulates Roman WFI Level-2 calibrated images over a chosen footprint, builds Level-3 skycell coadds and source catalogs from them, and measures how faithfully the recovered catalog reproduces the input catalog — a photometry recovery test. It was built to support simulated grism observations of a Wide-Field2 sub-region near (RA=10°, Dec=0°), but generalizes to any footprint and any input source catalog.
Code, configs, and this report live in the roman_l2_job GitHub repo.

title: roman_l2_job
text:
How this report is organized

Pipeline — what the pipeline does, how to install it, what each stage reads and writes, and the deliberate deviations from stock upstream (vendored make_stack.py, per-line deterministic seeds, CRDS hydration). Common to every campaign.
Full run — the first real campaign on configs/full.yaml, 324 sims across 6 visits of the (RA=10°, Dec=0°) field.

Design — pointings, parameters, measured zodi.
Results — per-skycell completeness, flux residual, astrometric residual, depth distribution.
Discussion — observations and caveats.

title: roman_l2_job
text:
Useful references

Roman WFI technical page — authoritative source for WFI per-bandpass zodi, detector specs, and other pre-launch numbers we compare against.

title: L3 detection-efficiency study
text:
A reproducible pipeline to measure the detection efficiency of the Roman L3 (MosaicPipeline) + SourceCatalogStep stack as a function of:

sky position (multiple pointing groups at different ecliptic latitudes → different zodiacal backgrounds),
source type (point sources vs exponential-profile galaxies, matched in position and magnitude),
source magnitude (F158, 23.0–25.0 in 0.1-mag bins),
SourceCatalogStep parameters (primarily kernel_fwhm).

The workflow is five phases with a user checkpoint at the end of each:

Pointing selection — pick a set of HLWAS F158 re-observed pairs spread across the sky, each with a well-defined solar-elongation window.
Skycell / SCA overlap analysis — find which skycells the pair geometries reach mosaic depth 6, and enumerate the (pointing, SCA) pairs that need to be simulated.
Source catalog generation — build matched star/galaxy truth catalogs on a grid inside each target skycell.
L2 + L3 simulation — run romanisim for each required (pointing, SCA) combination, build the skycell associations, and coadd with MosaicPipeline.
Source detection + efficiency analysis — run SourceCatalogStep at default parameters (5a), then sweep kernel_fwhm (5b) and optionally snr_threshold / npixels (5c). Compare recovered catalogs against the Phase-3 truth.

This sits alongside the existing Full run report and reuses the same roman_l2_job pixi environment, CRDS cache, and STPSF data.

The workflow is five phases with a user checkpoint at the end of each:

title: L3 detection-efficiency study
text:
Data locations
Code, configs, and selection artefacts live in this repo. The bulk L2 and L3 binary products — which will be the dominant cost once Phase 4 runs — will live under output/detection/ on the host, outside of version control. A pointer file will be added when Phase 4 starts.

title: L3 detection-efficiency study
text:
Status
Phases 1-3 complete. Phase 4a (single-skycell L2 + L3 validation on pair 11119) next.

Phase 1 — pointing selection
Phase 2 — skycell / SCA overlap
Phase 3 — truth-catalog generation

title: Phase 3 — truth-catalog generation
text:
For each skycell selected in Phase 2, generate two matched truth catalogs — one of point sources, one of galaxies — at identical RA/Dec and F158 magnitudes. The matched design lets us compare detection efficiency point-for-point between stars and galaxies of the same brightness at the same sky position, which is the core science question of the study.
Each catalog holds 210 sources = 21 magnitude bins × 10 sources per bin, magnitudes running 23.0–25.0 in 0.1-mag steps. Fluxes are stored as maggies in the F158 column (= 10^(-mag/2.5)) — the unit romanisim expects.
The galaxy catalog sets type="SER" with Sersic n=1, half_light_radius = 0.275", pa=0, ba=1 (circular exponential). The stars catalog sets type="PSF" (PSF-only).

title: Phase 3 — truth-catalog generation
text:
For each skycell selected in Phase 2, generate two matched truth catalogs — one of point sources, one of galaxies — at identical RA/Dec and F158 magnitudes. The matched design lets us compare detection efficiency point-for-point between stars and galaxies of the same brightness at the same sky position, which is the core science question of the study.
Each catalog holds 210 sources = 21 magnitude bins × 10 sources per bin, magnitudes running 23.0–25.0 in 0.1-mag steps. Fluxes are stored as maggies in the F158 column (= 10^(-mag/2.5)) — the unit romanisim expects.
The galaxy catalog sets type="SER" with Sersic n=1, half_light_radius = 0.275", pa=0, ba=1 (circular exponential). The stars catalog sets type="PSF" (PSF-only).

title: Phase 3 — truth-catalog generation
text:
Placement — Sobol quasi-random
Instead of a regular grid (which would correlate magnitude with pixel column and could interact with SCA boundaries or PSF variations), we use a 2-D Sobol low-discrepancy sequence inside the skycell’s unique core. The magnitude assignment is a separate seeded shuffle, so every mag bin’s 10 sources are scattered across the full skycell area — no systematic mag-vs-position correlation.
Skycells overlap their neighbours by skycell_border_pixels = 100 (per the Roman skymap reference file), so the unique core of each 5000×5000 skycell is [100, 4900]². We inject sources inside [200, 4800]² — a 200-pixel margin that puts every source 100 pixels inside the unique core, with room to spare for PSF wings and drizzle kernels.
With 210 sources in a 4600×4600 pix injection area at 0.055″/pix, mean nearest-neighbour separation is ~17″, well above the ~5–10″ blending threshold, so no rejection sampling is needed.





Pair 11119 skycell 000m18x58y45 source layout. LEFT: all 210 Sobol-distributed stars coloured by F158 magnitude; the dashed rectangle is the 200-pixel core boundary. RIGHT: same points re-rendered to highlight per-mag-bin coverage — notice each magnitude bin still samples the whole skycell.

title: Phase 3 — truth-catalog generation
text:
Reproducing
pixi run python scripts/detection/03_generate_catalogs.py
# Or, for the single-skycell validation on pair 11119:
pixi run python scripts/detection/03_generate_catalogs.py --only-pair 11119
Writes per-skycell parquet files under catalogs/detection/catalogs/skycell_<name>_{stars,galaxies}.parquet plus a catalogs_index.ecsv summary and a grid_<name>.png QA plot per skycell.

title: Phase 3 — truth-catalog generation
text:
Checkpoint
Phase 3 deliverable. Pair 11119 is the validation case — next step is Phase 4a: generate its 14 L2 files, coadd to a single L3 mosaic, and verify depth/coverage/image quality before scaling.

title: Full run — discussion
text: A handful of findings from this run are worth flagging for whoever uses the pipeline next — whether to follow up, to adjust the design of the next campaign, or to keep in mind when consuming the catalogs.

title: Full run — discussion
text:
Footprint non-uniformity around (10°, 0°)
Our target is a grism reference point near (RA = 10°, Dec = 0°), which sits at the northern survey boundary of the HLWAS simulation used to build the pointings. The default APT-planned tiling (at least in the version of the HLWAS .ecsv we filtered) does not uniformly cover this region — the filter_pointings.py cone pulls out three separated visit-clusters (segments 490 and 521) with shallow between-cluster gaps:

Only ~25% of skycells within 0.5° of the target reach the design depth of 6.
Only ~52 of 320 skycells have median_depth == 6; the bulk sit at median depth 2.

Options if we want better coverage of this field:

Move the default. If the grism target doesn’t have to be exactly (10°, 0°), picking a point ~0.5°–1° south would put it inside the survey interior where tiling is uniform. Downside: we’d lose any continuity with prior analyses tied to the current coordinates.
Add pointings. Two visits with the default dither pattern around (10°, 0°) would roughly double the existing coverage (+108 sims on top of the current 324) and fill the immediate neighbourhood without moving the target.
Go to a 3×3 grid. Nine pointings centered on the target is a clean geometry; budget is 9 × 3 × 18 = 486 new sims (~16 hr of stage 02 at current pace). To limit L3 work after that, we could run stages 04 and 05 only on skycells with sufficient asn-member count — e.g. len(asn.members) >= 14 correlates well with per-pixel depth ≥ 6 in the current data. That filter alone would drop stage-04 load by ~80% on the 3×3 footprint.

Whichever route we pick, the pipeline side of that work is straightforward: edit the config (new pointings.region centre, wider radius_deg for a grid) and re-run from stage 01. The skip-if-exists guards mean cal files from the current run will be reused for any overlapping pointings.

title: Full run — discussion
section: Zodiacal background: in MJy/sr, close to the Roman reference
text:
Zodiacal background: in MJy/sr, close to the Roman reference
What we measured. Sigma-clipped median on an F158 cal file (output/cal/r0000101015521001006_0001_wfi01_f158_cal.asdf), with detected sources masked (5σ threshold, ≥5 connected pixels):

0.65 DN/s/pix on the raw data array. romanisim.image.make_l2 documents this array as ramp slopes in DN/s.
0.50 MJy/sr via the cal file’s meta.photometry.conversion_megajanskys = 0.77.
1.28 e⁻/pix/s if we multiply DN/s by the WFI01 CRDS gain reference (median 1.99 e⁻/DN).

The 12-file sample averaged across visits and SCAs (median of medians) gives 0.67 DN/s/pix, i.e. 0.52 MJy/sr — consistent field-to-field within a few percent.
What we compared against. A Pandeia ETC run at (RA=10°, Dec=0°), F158, single 107.52 s exposure, returns an input zodi of 0.38 MJy/sr and a total background of 10.71 e⁻/s in a 51.93-pixel aperture → 0.206 e⁻/pix/s.
Result in MJy/sr. 0.50 vs 0.38 → 1.32× higher. That’s a modest model-vs-model difference between romanisim’s zodi model and Pandeia’s at the same coords and date, worth ~2.5·log₁₀(√1.32) ≈ 0.15 mag of 5σ point-source depth.
Result in e⁻/s. 1.28 vs 0.206 → 6.3×. This is not a real background excess. Cross-check: we aperture-photometered 30 isolated input point sources at AB 18.5–20.5 on the same cal file (3-pix circular aperture, annulus-subtracted sky, EE-corrected), multiplied by gain to get e⁻/s, and compared to the ETC zeropoint implied by its mag-24 example (1.42 e⁻/s in a 0.2″ aperture). The median measured/predicted ratio was 6.7× — matching the sky ratio to within 5%. Both source and sky carry the same offset in the e⁻/s reporting, so the source/sky ratio — and therefore SNR at fixed AB magnitude — is internally self-consistent within romanisim.
The likely origin of the 6–7× e⁻/s discrepancy is a convention difference between romanisim and Pandeia somewhere in the chain {bandpass integral, effective area, throughput, gain} — the two codes have to agree in MJy/sr (they roughly do) and in AB magnitudes (that we have not independently verified), but the e⁻/s-per-MJy/sr bridge between them is not constrained to match.
Cross-check against romanisim’s own sky model. romanisim.image (line ~697) injects sky using galsim.roman.getSkyLevel, scaled by (1 + stray_light_fraction) and with thermal_backgrounds[H158] added per pixel. Calling that chain directly at (RA=10°, Dec=0°), 2026-01-01:







term
value




getSkyLevel (zodi only)
95.01 e⁻/s/arcsec²


× (1 + stray_light_fraction = 1.1)
104.51 e⁻/s/arcsec²


× pixel area (0.11² = 0.0121 arcsec²)
1.265 e⁻/s/pix


+ thermal_backgrounds['H158'] = 0.040 e⁻/s/pix
1.305 e⁻/s/pix



That matches the cal-file measurement of 1.298 e⁻/s/pix to 0.5%, confirming the sim is faithfully injecting exactly what romanisim’s sky model predicts. Translating back to MJy/sr via the same romanisim-internal get_abflux(F158) / 3631 Jy × pix_area_sr conversion used above, 1.305 e⁻/s/pix corresponds to 0.493 MJy/sr, versus 0.502 measured — again consistent. So the ~30% MJy/sr gap against Pandeia’s 0.38 is a genuine zodi-model disagreement between the Hirata tables galsim uses and Pandeia’s model, not a sim setup issue.
Gain consistency, as a side effect. getSkyLevel is gain-free (it returns e⁻/s/arcsec² from a direct table lookup), but our cal-side number is DN/s × gain with gain = 1.994 e⁻/DN from the WFI01 CRDS reference (roman_wfi_gain_0022.asdf). The 0.5% agreement therefore verifies that the gain we multiply by on read matches the gain romanisim divided by when writing the DN/s slopes — a consistency check, not an absolute gain validation. (If the CRDS gain were physically off by 2× both sides would be wrong by 2× and still agree; that would need a separate absolute test, e.g. a ramp Poisson-noise consistency check.) In practice this is enough to trust DN/s × gain_CRDS as the correct way to recover e⁻/s from the cal file for any downstream analysis.
Upshot. Zodi is not responsible for the observed 0.5–1 mag depth shortfall. The earlier wording in this section — “3.4× the polar minimum → 0.67 mag of depth loss” — compared romanisim’s e⁻/s against a Pandeia-convention e⁻/s reference, which we now know is an apples-to-oranges comparison, and is retracted. The depth shortfall is still real and the explanation is still open.

title: Full run — discussion
section: psf_flux appears to be in different units than aper08_flux / kron_flux
text:
psf_flux appears to be in different units than aper08_flux / kron_flux
For input PSF sources, we expect psf_flux ≈ aper08_flux (both capture total flux for point sources). Measured offset: psf_mag − aper08_mag = +0.656 mag (input-PSF sources, psf_flags = 0). The matching kron − aper08 is +0.07 for the same sources, so kron and aper08 agree well.
Grepping romancal.source_catalog.psf, psf_flux comes straight from a photutils.psf.PSFPhotometry fit on image_model.data with the flux_fit column renamed — no unit conversion is applied on the way out. aper*_flux and kron_flux in the same catalog output, in contrast, are in nJy (we confirmed kron_flux round-trips with kron_abmag via the standard AB formula to 1 part in 10⁴).
The systematic offset matches the median CRDS-gain across the 18 WFI SCAs (1.869 e⁻/DN → 2.5 × log₁₀ = +0.679 mag) to ~2%. Our working hypothesis is that psf_flux is in DN/s-like units while aper_flux and kron_flux have been converted to nJy. The 2% residual may come from (a) per-SCA gain variation, since L3 coadds are weighted combinations of contributing L2s, or (b) PSF-wing truncation in the 8-pixel aperture, which would push the sign the other way, so probably (a) dominates.
Consequences for downstream science:

Do not use psf_flux as a standalone total flux estimator until the unit chain is verified. Use kron_flux, which is internally self-consistent in this catalog, for flux comparisons.
Worth filing upstream against romancal to either (a) apply the gain correction to psf_flux in the same pipeline stage that converts aperture/Kron fluxes, or (b) document the difference in unit explicitly.

title: Full run — discussion
text:
Depth quantization to even integers
For context on the Results depth-distribution plot — per-pixel depths in this run occur only at even integers (2, 4, 6, 8). That is not a coverage artifact: passes 15 and 16 of the HLWAS design re-observe each visit with identical (RA, Dec, PA) and dither offsets, so a pixel sees depth k in pass 15 and depth k in pass 16 — the sum is always 2k. The smoke config (1 visit × 3 dithers, no second pass) did see odd depths 1/2/3; the full config shouldn’t.

title: Full run — discussion
text:
Kron flux bias (+0.17 mag)
The median recovered Kron − input mag of +0.17 mag is stable across depth, skycell, and the smoke-vs-full comparison. It’s a well-known Kron property on extended sources — Kron radius is self-sizing and catches ~90% of a Sérsic profile’s light, not 100%. For input PSF sources the Kron residual is much smaller (median +0.07 mag on a deep skycell check). This is a photometry property, not a pipeline calibration issue.
If you want an unbiased recovered flux for galaxies specifically, segment_flux or a larger aperture (e.g. aper16_flux) would be the better choice.

title: Full run — results
text:
Note



Numbers on this page are computed at render time from output/full/compare/summary.csv and the coadd .asdf files, so a re-rendered report picks up any re-run results.

title: Full run — results
text:
Configuration
324 sims over 6 visits (2 passes × 3 dithers × segments 490 + 521), 320 skycells after skycell_asn --product-type full. The design depth for this run is pointings.design_depth = 6 (both passes × all 3 dithers); the “design subset” below picks input sources whose (ra, dec) lands on a coadd pixel of depth ≥ 6.

title: Full run — results
text:
Depth distribution





Distribution of per-skycell depth metrics across the full run. Left: the maximum per-pixel depth reached in each skycell. Right: the median per-pixel depth (a proxy for how well each skycell is covered overall).

title: Full run — results
section: How to read a per-skycell figure
text:
How to read a per-skycell figure
Each per-skycell PNG has six panels. In brief:

Detection completeness (top-left). Binned completeness vs input magnitude. The black curve is all in-footprint sources; the dashed red curve is the “design subset” (sources on pixels with depth ≥ 6). PSF and SER inputs are shown faintly for context. Dotted verticals mark the interpolated 50%-completeness magnitudes.
Flux residual (top-middle). recovered kron mag − input mag vs input mag, matched sources only. Grey = sources below design depth, red = design-depth sources. The red dashed line is the design-subset median bias (typically +0.15–0.20 mag, a Kron-photometry property on extended sources).
PSF vs aper08 (top-right). psf_mag − aper08_mag vs aper08_mag, for recovered sources with psf_flags = 0, coloured by the input type: blue = input PSF, orange = input SER. Input PSFs should concentrate near Δ = 0 (PSF fit and aperture agree on point sources); input SERs should sit above Δ = 0 (PSF-fit underestimates extended flux). Caveat: in these catalogs we see both clouds shifted up by ~+0.68 mag — the PSF cloud sits near Δ ≈ +0.66 instead of 0, and the SER cloud near Δ ≈ +2.6 instead of ~+2. That shift matches the median CRDS gain (1.869 e⁻/DN → 2.5·log₁₀(1.869) = +0.68 mag), which strongly suggests psf_flux is emitted in DN/s-like units while aper08_flux and kron_flux are in nJy. We leave the panel uncorrected so the offset is visible; use kron_flux (which round-trips with kron_abmag) for any direct quantitative comparison rather than psf_flux until this is clarified upstream.
False-positive rate (bottom-left). Fraction of recovered sources per recovered-mag bin that have no matching input within the match radius. Spurious detections / blends; usually well below 10% except at the faint end.
Astrometric residual (bottom-middle). |Δpos| between matched recovered and input positions. Roman WFI is astrometrically very tight — typically ~0.015–0.020″, sub-coadd-pixel, across the full input-mag range.
Metadata panel (bottom-right). Skycell identifiers, coadd shape, depth statistics, and headline numbers for both the “all” and “design” sub-populations.

title: Full run — results
text:
Aggregated depth distribution
Pixel count per depth across every valid pixel of every skycell, with the cumulative fraction at depth ≥ X overlaid. Read directly off the red curve: the fraction of pixels meeting a given depth — e.g., at the design-depth threshold of 6 for this run, ~21% of pixels qualify.

title: Full run — design
text: This configuration was built to support simulated grism observations of a Wide-Field2 sub-region near (RA = 10°, Dec = 0°), at four position angles (PA = 0°, 10°, 170°, 180°). We need imaging coverage of the grism reference position and a few arcminutes along the dispersion direction so that source positions measured from imaging constrain the spectral trace extraction.

title: Full run — design
text: This configuration was built to support simulated grism observations of a Wide-Field2 sub-region near (RA = 10°, Dec = 0°), at four position angles (PA = 0°, 10°, 170°, 180°). We need imaging coverage of the grism reference position and a few arcminutes along the dispersion direction so that source positions measured from imaging constrain the spectral trace extraction.

title: Full run — design
text:
Footprint filter
The pointings file (pointings_full.ecsv) is a subset of catalogs/HLWAS.sim.ecsv (the full HLWAS-survey simulated pointings) produced by scripts/filter_pointings.py:


region.type      = cone
region.ra        = 10.0°
region.dec       = 0.0°
region.radius    = 0.5°
bandpass         = F158
only_pass        = None
only_segment     = None
only_visit       = None


A visit survives the filter if any of its dithers lands inside the cone — matching visits contribute all their dithers (no partial inclusion). The only_pass/segment/visit nulls here mean no further restriction after the region test; the smoke config sets these to a single-visit subset.
The selection yields 6 visits × 3 dithers = 18 exposures across passes 15 and 16, segments 490 and 521:






The 18 pointings selected by the full-run filter.

Passes 15 and 16 share the same (RA, Dec, PA) for every exposure — they are re-observations, not distinct pointings. That matters for interpreting per-pixel depth: the two passes stack at identical dither patterns, so depth contributions from them are always paired.

title: Full run — design
text:
Pointings map (full HLWAS footprint)





The full HLWAS F158 footprint (grey dots = all ~67k pointings) with our target (red star) marked and the 0.5° filter cone drawn. The simulated survey sits south of Dec ≈ +1°, so our target at (10°, 0°) is near the northern edge — any roughly-isotropic filter cone drawn around it will necessarily have a lopsided pointing sample.




The HLWAS F158 footprint as simulated runs from Dec ≈ −53° to Dec ≈ +3°, with a hard upper edge around Dec ≈ +1° where the survey planning truncates. Our target at (10°, 0°) sits ~1° from that edge — which is why wide-area maps around it look asymmetric in Dec. It is not an artifact of our filter.

title: Full run — design
text:
Pointings map (wide view)





HLWAS F158 pointings near our field. Grey dots are the full HLWAS F158 catalog in a 5° window around our target; red + are the 18 selected for the full run; the 0.5° filter cone is drawn as a dashed circle; the grism target (10°, 0°) is the red star.




Zooming in, the northern truncation from the full-footprint view above is still visible as the thinning of grey points above Dec = 0°.

title: Full run — design
text:
Pointings map (close-up)





Close-up around the 0.5° filter cone. The 18 selected pointings form three visit-clusters — one from segment 490 near (9.6°, −0.1°), and two from segment 521 near (10.5°, 0°) and (10.1°, +0.2°).




Each of the three visit-clusters covers its own ~1 WFI footprint; the gaps between clusters are covered shallowly, which has consequences for per-skycell coverage.

title: Full run — design
text:
Skycell coverage map





Skycell centres from the full run, coloured by per-skycell median per-pixel depth. The design depth for this config is 6 (all 3 dithers of both passes); it is reached only in the cluster cores, not across the full filter cone.

title: Full run — design
text:
Measured zodiacal background
Per-bandpass expected zodi rates for Roman WFI are documented on the Roman WFI technical page. We quote the measurement below primarily in MJy/sr — that’s the unit a Pandeia ETC consumes as input and the unit romanisim injects zodi in internally, and it’s therefore the apples-to-apples unit for comparison. The DN/s and e⁻/s columns are reported for reference; see the Discussion note on the e⁻/s-convention mismatch between romanisim and Pandeia.





Sigma-clipped sky rate per SCA from one exposure. MJy/sr uses each SCA’s meta.photometry.conversion_megajanskys; e⁻/s uses the median CRDS gain ≈ 1.87 e⁻/DN.

Mean across 18 SCAs: 0.481 MJy/sr  (range 0.446 – 0.501)
Field at (RA=10, Dec=0): ecliptic latitude -3.96°
Solar elongation on sim date: 128.2°


A Pandeia ETC run for the same filter, coords and date gives an input zodi of 0.38 MJy/sr, so romanisim’s sky is ~30% higher than Pandeia’s model for this field. Both codes place the field on the ecliptic (latitude −4°) at a large solar elongation (~128°), which is a structurally bright but well-observed part of the sky for a Dec ≈ 0 target; within Roman’s elongation constraint (~54°–126°), the sim date is already near the upper end of the window, so shifting dates cannot meaningfully lower the background.

title: Full run — design

Data volume and wall time
Disk and wall-time cost of the full run. The pipeline is configured to use output/cal/ as a shared store (same filenames per visit/exposure/SCA), so cal files carry over between smoke and full — only the novel L2s were newly simulated for this run.

Two small inefficiencies worth knowing about:

output/full/mosaic/ contains duplicates of the catalog outputs (_cat.parquet, _segm.asdf) that MosaicPipeline writes as a side-effect of its internal source-catalog substep; stage 05 then regenerates them into output/full/catalog/. The side-effect copies account for ~24 GB of the mosaic-dir total.
Most of the L3-catalog volume is _segm.asdf; the actual source catalogs (_cat.parquet) are tiny (~74 MB for all 320).

The EBS volume grew from 259 GB (initial) to 415 GB mid-run when the mosaic step outgrew the original allocation. Current usage after the full run is ~300 GB / 415 GB (~73%).

title: Pipeline
text: The pipeline is a linear chain of stages driven by a YAML config. The same stages and the same code are used for every campaign; only the config changes (see the Full run section for the config used in this report). This page documents what the pipeline is, how to install it, and what each stage does. Campaign-specific numbers live in the per-campaign pages.

title: Pipeline
text:
What it does
Starting from:

an input source catalog (maggies per bandpass, ra/dec, type)
a pointings file carved out of catalogs/HLWAS.sim.ecsv by a cone- or box-shaped footprint filter

the pipeline produces:

L2 cal files: one per (exposure × SCA), simulated with romanisim-make-image under the real CRDS reference chain.
L3 skycell coadds and associations: Roman skycells that overlap the L2 footprint, coadded with romancal.pipeline.MosaicPipeline.
Source catalogs: per-mosaic detections + photometry via romancal.source_catalog.SourceCatalogStep.
Recovery diagnostics: per-skycell completeness, flux residual, astrometric residual, FP rate, plus an aggregated depth histogram — computed by scripts/06_compare_catalog.py.

title: Pipeline
text:
Installation
The project is managed by pixi. Once-only setup on a fresh Linux x86_64 host:
git clone git@github.com:roman-grs-pit/roman_l2_job.git
cd roman_l2_job

# install the default env (romanisim, romancal, stpsf, etc.)
pixi install

# install the report env (quarto + plotting stack)
pixi install --environment report

# download STPSF data + scaffold CRDS directory
pixi run bash scripts/00_setup.sh

# pre-populate the CRDS reference cache (serial, avoids parallel races)
pixi run bash scripts/00_hydrate_crds.sh configs/smoke.yaml

# copy your input catalog into place (not tracked by git)
scp metadata.parquet user@host:roman_l2_job/data/

# prepare the working catalog (mag → maggies if needed)
pixi run python scripts/00_prepare_catalog.py configs/smoke.yaml
The env pins Python 3.12 + pinned-version romanisim, romancal, and stpsf. See pixi.toml for the dependency list. BLAS threads are pinned to 1 in the activation env so parallel xargs -P workers don’t oversubscribe the box.

title: Pipeline
text:
Pipeline shape
Every numbered stage is a shell script (or Python) taking configs/<tag>.yaml as its single argument. Outputs are idempotent — each stage skips items that already exist, so re-running picks up partial state for free.









Stage
Script
Reads
Writes




setup
00_setup.sh
—
STPSF data, CRDS dir


hydrate CRDS
00_hydrate_crds.sh <config>
CRDS server
populated crds_cache/


verify CRDS
00_verify_crds.py
crds_cache/
exit nonzero on size outliers


catalog prep
00_prepare_catalog.py <config>
catalog.input
catalogs/sources.parquet (maggies)


filter pointings
filter_pointings.py <config>
catalogs/HLWAS.sim.ecsv
pointings_<tag>.ecsv


01 build
01_build_script.sh <config>
pointings + sources
output/<tag>/sims.script


02 sims
02_run_sims.sh <config>
sims.script
output/cal/*_cal.asdf


03 asn
03_build_asn.sh <config>
matching cal files
output/<tag>/asn/*.json


04 mosaic
04_run_mosaic.sh <config>
asn jsons
output/<tag>/mosaic/*_coadd.asdf


05 catalog
05_run_catalog.sh <config>
mosaics
output/<tag>/catalog/*_cat.parquet + *_segm.asdf


06 compare
06_compare_catalog.py <config>
sources, coadds, recovered catalogs
output/<tag>/compare/{plots/*.png, summary.csv, depth_distribution.png}



Stages 02, 04, and 05 use xargs -P $PARALLELISM (default taken from the config’s run.parallelism, env var PARALLELISM overrides).

title: Pipeline
text:
Configuration
A minimal config (the actual configs/full.yaml):
tag: full

catalog:
  input: data/metadata.parquet
  input_units: mag            # or "maggies" (no-op the conversion)
  bandpass_col: F158

pointings:
  region:
    type: cone                # or "box" with ra_min/max + dec_min/max
    ra: 10.0
    dec: 0.0
    radius_deg: 0.5
  bandpass: F158
  only_pass: null             # optional visit filter, null = no restriction
  only_segment: null
  only_visit: null

run:
  parallelism: 4
Every field is required — no hidden defaults in code. only_pass/segment/visit accept integer values to carve out sub-selections (the smoke config uses them to pick a single-visit subset for quick shake-downs).
scripts/_config.py is the loader. It validates the schema and exposes a shell-export mode so bash stages can read values via eval "$(pixi run python scripts/_config.py $CONFIG)".

title: Pipeline
text:
Design choices
A few deliberate deviations from upstream stock romanisim / romancal worth knowing about:

Vendored make_stack.py. Upstream romanisim-make-stack unconditionally dereferences apt_metadata even in the no-APT path, which crashes the script when --apt isn’t supplied. scripts/make_stack.py is a verbatim copy with one 3-line guard added. Delete when upstream merges a fix.
Per-line deterministic seeds. romanisim-make-stack --make_script bakes the same --rng_seed into every generated line, defeating noise realization between dithers. scripts/_postprocess_sims.py rewrites each seed to crc32(output_basename) — reproducible, unique per (visit × exposure × SCA).
CRDS hydrate + verify. The original smoke run crashed because a worker was OOM-killed mid-CRDS-download, leaving a truncated reference file that then broke every subsequent sim. 00_hydrate_crds.sh pre-fetches every CRDS reference our pipeline uses (14 reftypes, serially via crds.getreferences) so the parallel stage-02 run never races on downloads; 00_verify_crds.py is a size-outlier check that stage 02 runs as a pre-flight.
Parallelism. PARALLELISM=4 on m5a.2xlarge, not the default nproc, because the 8 vCPUs are SMT threads over 4 physical cores and 8-way romanisim spikes to ~62 GB RAM on a 32 GB box. Stage 04 uses the same value for strun MosaicPipeline workers.
Footprint mask = weight > 0. The recovery analysis trusts only sources whose (ra, dec) project onto a valid pixel of the coadd, so edge-of-footprint non-detections don’t count against completeness.

For full operator detail (sizing, ETA, memory, troubleshooting recipes), see CLAUDE.md in the repo. For file-format specifics (recovered catalog columns, coadd array names, CRDS dataset dict shape), see docs/data_model_reference.md.

title: Phase 2 — skycell / SCA overlap
text:
Phase 2 is purely geometric — no image data is simulated. For each of the 30 pointings from Phase 1, we:

Build the 18-SCA WCS at the pointing (via galsim.roman.getWCS(world_pos, SCAs=None, date, PA)), which applies the SIAF field-of-view offsets and the Roman distortion model to produce a GalSim CelestialWCS per SCA.
Extract the four corner positions of each 4088×4088 SCA.
Call romancal.skycell.match.find_skycell_matches(corners) to get the list of skycells the SCA footprint overlaps — this walks the skymap KDTree and tests polygon overlap against each candidate projection region’s skycell grid.

From the 540 (pointing, SCA) → skycell(s) triples we build two aggregate tables and pick one depth-6 skycell per pair for Phase 3/4.

title: Phase 2 — skycell / SCA overlap
text:
Phase 2 is purely geometric — no image data is simulated. For each of the 30 pointings from Phase 1, we:

Build the 18-SCA WCS at the pointing (via galsim.roman.getWCS(world_pos, SCAs=None, date, PA)), which applies the SIAF field-of-view offsets and the Roman distortion model to produce a GalSim CelestialWCS per SCA.
Extract the four corner positions of each 4088×4088 SCA.
Call romancal.skycell.match.find_skycell_matches(corners) to get the list of skycells the SCA footprint overlaps — this walks the skymap KDTree and tests polygon overlap against each candidate projection region’s skycell grid.

From the 540 (pointing, SCA) → skycell(s) triples we build two aggregate tables and pick one depth-6 skycell per pair for Phase 3/4.

title: Phase 2 — skycell / SCA overlap

Overlap counts
Every pair has ≥ 1 skycell where all 6 pointings overlap (depth 6, the design maximum); depth falls off near the edges of the pair’s combined footprint.

title: Phase 2 — skycell / SCA overlap
text:
Per-pair coverage maps
Each panel shows one pair. Coloured polygons are skycells in the (pair, skycell) table — fill colour encodes mosaic depth (0 → max). Thin outlines are the 18 SCA footprints of each of the pair’s 6 pointings, colour-coded by pointing. The red thick outline is the Phase-2-selected skycell for that pair (the deepest one nearest the pair’s nominal centre). The black + is the pair centre.

The selected skycells
One depth-6 skycell per pair — selected to be the deepest skycell nearest the pair’s nominal centre.

text:
Reproducing
pixi run python scripts/detection/02_skycell_overlap.py
Writes:

title: Phase 2 — skycell / SCA overlap
text:
Checkpoint
Phase 2 deliverable. Before Phase 3, we need to pick a storage location with the user for the L2/L3 binary products (they won’t live in the repo). Default: output/detection/{l2,l3}/ in-tree, with the corresponding subpaths added to .gitignore.

title: Phase 1 — pointing selection
text:
The candidate pool is catalogs/HLWAS.sim.ecsv — the simulated Roman High-Latitude Wide-Area Survey pointing list. We apply these filters to arrive at the selection pool:

BANDPASS == "F158".
Exactly two visits share identical (RA, Dec, PA) at 10⁻⁵ deg precision — these are the survey-design re-observations. Visit centres are computed with a circular mean over the 3 dither RAs (important near RA = 0°/360°: a linear mean would place a dither triplet straddling the wrap at a phantom RA ≈ 120°/240°).
The two visits are from PASS = 15 and PASS = 16 — the dominant revisit mode in HLWAS.
MA_TABLE_NUMBER == 1007 (the 107.52 s F158 imaging cadence; the only MA table used by the (15,16) re-observations).
Interior check: at least 200 F158 exposures sit within 2° of the pair centre. Drops phantom centres and near-edge pairs.

Selection then picks from this pool:

Two anchors: at RA ∈ [10°, 30°], one at Dec ≈ −35° and one at Dec ≈ −2°. These sit above/below the main survey body and give the final selection a broader ecliptic-latitude range than any single Dec slice can.
Three RA-spread picks from the main box RA ∈ [330°, 360°] ∪ [0°, 50°], Dec ∈ [−25°, −10°]: bin the interior-pool pairs in this box into 3 equal-count bins along the wrap-aware RA axis, and in each bin take the median-Dec candidate.

After filtering, every visit has exactly 3 dithered exposures, PA is uniform within a pair (both visits share a roll), and the nominal RA/Dec centres are pairwise identical. Each pair therefore gives 6 dithered exposures at one (RA, Dec, PA) with two different observation dates.






Funnel from the full HLWAS table down to the candidate pool for selection.

title: Phase 1 — pointing selection
text:
The candidate pool is catalogs/HLWAS.sim.ecsv — the simulated Roman High-Latitude Wide-Area Survey pointing list. We apply these filters to arrive at the selection pool:

BANDPASS == "F158".
Exactly two visits share identical (RA, Dec, PA) at 10⁻⁵ deg precision — these are the survey-design re-observations. Visit centres are computed with a circular mean over the 3 dither RAs (important near RA = 0°/360°: a linear mean would place a dither triplet straddling the wrap at a phantom RA ≈ 120°/240°).
The two visits are from PASS = 15 and PASS = 16 — the dominant revisit mode in HLWAS.
MA_TABLE_NUMBER == 1007 (the 107.52 s F158 imaging cadence; the only MA table used by the (15,16) re-observations).
Interior check: at least 200 F158 exposures sit within 2° of the pair centre. Drops phantom centres and near-edge pairs.

Selection then picks from this pool:

Two anchors: at RA ∈ [10°, 30°], one at Dec ≈ −35° and one at Dec ≈ −2°. These sit above/below the main survey body and give the final selection a broader ecliptic-latitude range than any single Dec slice can.
Three RA-spread picks from the main box RA ∈ [330°, 360°] ∪ [0°, 50°], Dec ∈ [−25°, −10°]: bin the interior-pool pairs in this box into 3 equal-count bins along the wrap-aware RA axis, and in each bin take the median-Dec candidate.

After filtering, every visit has exactly 3 dithered exposures, PA is uniform within a pair (both visits share a roll), and the nominal RA/Dec centres are pairwise identical. Each pair therefore gives 6 dithered exposures at one (RA, Dec, PA) with two different observation dates.

title: Phase 1 — pointing selection
text:
Solar-elongation window
Each selected pair is simulated on a date where the solar elongation of the field is in [90°, 115°], as close to 90° as possible — minimum elongation drives maximum zodiacal brightness, which makes the recovery test conservative (hardest detection), while staying inside Roman’s viewable-zone constraint. The chosen date minimises (elong − 90°) while staying inside the window.





Solar elongation vs day of year 2026 for the five selected pairs. Grey band = [90°, 115°]; filled dot = the chosen simulation date (closest to 90° inside the window). Each pair has two windows per year; we pick the first-hit day for reproducibility.

title: Phase 1 — pointing selection
text:
The selection
Two anchor picks (southern and northern edge, Dec ≈ −35° and Dec ≈ −2° at RA ≈ 20°) plus three RA-spread picks in the main box at median Dec.





The five selected pairs. SIM_DATE is the chosen simulation date for both visits in the pair. SKY_E_PER_PIX_PER_S is the romanisim-predicted F158 zodiacal sky at the pair’s (RA, Dec, date), including the 10% stray-light term and the 0.04 e⁻/pix/s F158 thermal background. WINDOWS_DOY lists all 2026 day-of-year ranges where the field’s solar elongation is inside [90°, 115°].

The set spans ecliptic latitudes from −9.5° to −39.6° (via the two anchors) while keeping every pair deep inside the HLWAS footprint. The predicted F158 zodiacal sky rate varies by a factor of ~2 across the five pairs.

title: Phase 1 — pointing selection
text:
Sky distribution





HLWAS F158 exposures (grey cloud), (15,16) interior pairs (blue), and the five selected pairs (red stars, annotated by PAIR_ID). Dashed black rectangle = the main-survey RA-spread box; dotted orange rectangles = the two anchor windows (southern and northern edge).

title: Phase 1 — pointing selection
text:
Predicted sky background
For each selected pair we also compute the expected F158 zodiacal sky rate using galsim.roman.getSkyLevel, matching the model romanisim uses internally (the same code path as romanisim-make-image at romanisim.image.simulate_counts:697). We include the 10% stray-light fraction and the 0.04 e⁻/pix/s F158 thermal background, and multiply by the Roman WFI pixel area (0.11″ native).
As a sanity check, romanisim at the full-run target (RA 10°, Dec 0°, sim-date 2026-01-01) predicts ~1.27 e⁻/pix/s here — matching the measured 1.28 e⁻/pix/s from the full-run L2 files.





Predicted F158 sky rate (e⁻/pix/s) for every interior pair at the elongation-optimal date, coloured by pair. The five selected pairs are over-plotted as red stars. Sky level tracks |ecliptic latitude| — the southern anchor (Pair 2550, ecl_lat -39.6°) is near the minimum; the northern anchor (Pair 2525, ecl_lat -9.5°) is near the maximum.

title: Phase 1 — pointing selection
text:
Reproducing
pixi run python scripts/detection/01_select_pointings.py
Writes catalogs/detection/selected_pairs.ecsv (pair-level, 10 rows), catalogs/detection/selected_pointings.ecsv (per-dither, 30 rows — the Phase 2 input), and the two diagnostic PNGs under output/detection/phase1/.

title: Phase 1 — pointing selection
text:
Checkpoint
Phase 1 deliverable — ready for user review before starting Phase 2 (skycell / SCA overlap analysis, no new simulations).
